title: Implement conversation metrics for activity, completion, duration, and errors
purpose: Instrument the conversation subsystem with the four metrics that operators need to monitor conversation health: active count (gauge), completion rate (counter), duration (histogram), and error rate (counter). These metrics surface conversation lifecycle events — start, complete, fail — as quantitative signals for capacity planning and failure detection.
task: Create crates/liminal/src/metrics/conversation.rs containing the ConversationMetrics struct that registers the four conversation metric families with the MetricsRegistry. Provide methods for the conversation actor to call on conversation start, completion, failure, and timeout events. Duration is recorded at completion time as the elapsed wall-clock time from conversation start. All recording is non-blocking.

requirements.title: Define ConversationMetrics struct
requirements.spec: ConversationMetrics SHALL hold handles to the four metric families: active_count (Gauge), completion_count (Counter), duration (Histogram), error_count (Counter). ConversationMetrics SHALL be constructed from a MetricsRegistry reference. ConversationMetrics SHALL implement Debug and Clone.
acceptance: ConversationMetrics struct exists in crates/liminal/src/metrics/conversation.rs
ConversationMetrics has fields for all four metric handles
ConversationMetrics::new(registry: &MetricsRegistry) registers all four families
ConversationMetrics implements Debug and Clone

files: crates/liminal/src/metrics/conversation.rs:new — target module to create for `ConversationMetrics` and `ConversationOutcome`
crates/liminal/src/metrics/channel.rs:1-47 — existing metrics wrapper pattern: constants, `#[derive(Clone, Debug)]`, fields, constructor registering metric families
crates/liminal/src/metrics/registry.rs:118-224 — direct `register_counter`, `register_gauge`, and `register_histogram` APIs returning atomic handles
crates/liminal/src/metrics/registry/families.rs:232-266 — family registration API used by `ChannelMetrics`, useful but has label/cache implications
crates/liminal/src/metrics/registry/collectors.rs:83-175 — concrete `CounterHandle`, `GaugeHandle`, and `HistogramHandle` methods and value types
context: Existing `ChannelMetrics` stores `CounterFamily`, `GaugeFamily`, and `HistogramFamily` because channel metrics are per-channel labelled; conversation metrics are global per the boundaries, so direct `CounterHandle`/`GaugeHandle`/`HistogramHandle` are a better fit for non-blocking hot-path recording.

`MetricsRegistry::register_*` returns `Result<*Handle, MetricRegistrationError>` and all public metric wrappers in this area derive `Clone, Debug`. The constructor should return `Result<Self, MetricRegistrationError>` like `ChannelMetrics::new`.

Metric names in channel metrics use lowercase snake-case constants like `channel_message_rate`; conversation names should follow the same convention, e.g. `conversation_active_count`, `conversation_completion_count`, `conversation_duration`, `conversation_error_count`.

There is a spec conflict: R1 says `ConversationMetrics::new(registry: &MetricsRegistry)`, while R4 says bucket boundaries are caller-supplied and no defaults are allowed. Current registry requires histogram buckets at registration time.
scout.approach: Create `crates/liminal/src/metrics/conversation.rs` with `use std::time::Duration;` and imports from `super::{CounterHandle, GaugeHandle, HistogramHandle, MetricRegistrationError, MetricsRegistry}`. Define metric-name constants, `#[derive(Clone, Debug)] pub struct ConversationMetrics { pub active_count: GaugeHandle, pub completion_count: CounterHandle, pub duration: HistogramHandle, pub error_count: CounterHandle }`, and a constructor that registers one unlabeled gauge, two unlabeled counters, and one unlabeled histogram. Prefer a signature that accepts duration buckets, e.g. `new(registry: &MetricsRegistry, duration_buckets: Vec<u64>) -> Result<Self, MetricRegistrationError>`, because the no-default-buckets boundary cannot be satisfied by `new(registry)` alone.
scout.notes: Do not blindly use `CounterFamily`/`GaugeFamily`/`HistogramFamily` for these global metrics: family `.increment(label)` and `.observe(label, ...)` go through a label-handle cache protected by `RwLock` on the hot path. If tests or the brief author insist on “families,” pre-resolving a single family label during construction would still leave public fields as families and future direct family calls could violate R8; direct handles are safer.

files_changed.note: Defines ConversationMetrics with active_count, completion_count, duration, and error_count direct handles plus a constructor registering the four global metric collectors.

dev.how: Created ConversationMetrics with public direct handle fields for the active gauge, completion counter, duration histogram, and error counter. The constructor registers unlabeled global metrics from a MetricsRegistry and requires caller-supplied duration buckets so no default histogram boundaries are imposed; ConversationMetrics derives Clone and Debug.

checklist.note: active_count is registered as a global GaugeHandle.
completion_count is registered as a global CounterHandle.
duration is registered as a global HistogramHandle with caller-provided buckets.
error_count is registered as a global CounterHandle.

acceptance.evidence: crates/liminal/src/metrics/conversation.rs:34-51 defines new with registry: &MetricsRegistry and registers conversation_active_count, conversation_completion_count, conversation_duration, and conversation_error_count. The constructor also accepts caller-provided duration_buckets to satisfy R4's no-default-buckets requirement.

requirements.title: Track active conversation count as a gauge
requirements.spec: ConversationMetrics SHALL expose record_start() that increments the active_count gauge by 1, and record_end() that decrements it by 1. The gauge SHALL reflect the current number of in-flight conversations across all channels. Both operations SHALL be atomic.
acceptance: record_start() increments active_count by 1
record_end() decrements active_count by 1
After 5 starts and 2 ends, gauge value is 3
Both methods are infallible

files: crates/liminal/src/metrics/conversation.rs:new — implement `record_start` and `record_end` here
crates/liminal/src/metrics/registry/collectors.rs:114-149 — `GaugeHandle` uses `AtomicI64` with `fetch_add`, `fetch_sub`, and `value`
crates/liminal/src/metrics/channel.rs:53-63 — existing gauge increment/decrement/set naming for subscribe/unsubscribe/queue depth
crates/liminal/src/metrics/registry.rs:95-100 — snapshots represent gauges as `MetricValue::Gauge(i64)`
context: `GaugeHandle::increment()` and `GaugeHandle::decrement()` are infallible and atomic; they do not return `Result`.
The existing channel wrapper exposes simple verb methods without `#[must_use]` or error returns; match that style for `record_start`/`record_end`.

Gauge values are signed `i64`, so repeated `record_end` without matching starts can drive the metric negative. The brief does not ask for guard logic, and guarding would add complexity/hot-path state.
scout.approach: Implement `pub fn record_start(&self) { self.active_count.increment(); }` and `pub fn record_end(&self) { self.active_count.decrement(); }` using the direct `GaugeHandle`. Add unit coverage in the new module that constructs a registry/metrics instance, calls `record_start` five times and `record_end` twice, then checks `metrics.active_count.value() == 3` or inspects the registry snapshot.
scout.notes: Do not clamp the active gauge at zero unless a later requirement says so; clamping would require a compare/update policy not present in the current `GaugeHandle` API and may hide lifecycle bugs.

files_changed.note: Adds infallible record_start and record_end methods backed by atomic GaugeHandle increment/decrement.
Covers the 5-start/2-end active gauge behavior and snapshot visibility.

dev.how: record_start increments the active conversation gauge and record_end decrements it. Both methods are infallible one-line direct handle calls and the integration test verifies that five starts followed by two ends leaves the gauge at 3.

checklist.note: Active conversations are tracked as a global gauge reflecting currently in-flight conversations.

stories.note: Operators can inspect active conversation count through the metrics registry snapshot.

acceptance.criterion: record_start() increments active_count by 1
acceptance.evidence: crates/liminal/src/metrics/conversation.rs:61-63 calls self.active_count.increment(); crates/liminal/tests/conversation_metrics.rs:28-35 exercises starts and observes value.

acceptance.criterion: record_end() decrements active_count by 1
acceptance.evidence: crates/liminal/src/metrics/conversation.rs:65-67 calls self.active_count.decrement(); crates/liminal/tests/conversation_metrics.rs:31-35 exercises two ends and observes value.

acceptance.criterion: After 5 starts and 2 ends, gauge value is 3
acceptance.evidence: Test active_count_tracks_open_conversations at crates/liminal/tests/conversation_metrics.rs:23-44 performs five record_start calls, two record_end calls, then asserts active_count.value() == 3 and registry snapshot Gauge(3).

acceptance.criterion: Both methods are infallible
acceptance.evidence: crates/liminal/src/metrics/conversation.rs:61-67 shows record_start and record_end return () and contain no Result-returning operations.

requirements.title: Track conversation completion rate as a counter
requirements.spec: ConversationMetrics SHALL expose record_completion() that increments the completion_count counter by 1. This is called when a conversation reaches a terminal success state. The counter SHALL be monotonically increasing.
acceptance: record_completion() increments completion_count by 1
Calling record_completion 10 times results in counter value 10
record_completion is infallible

files: crates/liminal/src/metrics/conversation.rs:new — implement `record_completion` here
crates/liminal/src/metrics/registry/collectors.rs:83-111 — `CounterHandle` saturating increment and `value` API
crates/liminal/src/metrics/channel.rs:49-51 — existing counter-recording wrapper method style
crates/liminal/src/metrics/registry.rs:95-99 — snapshots represent counters as `MetricValue::Counter(u64)`
context: Counters are `AtomicU64`; `increment()` delegates to `increment_by(1)` and saturates on overflow rather than panicking.
The method should be infallible and should not take outcome/duration parameters; terminal aggregation belongs to R6.
Existing metric wrappers keep recording methods tiny one-liners; use the same style for clarity and to help reviewers verify R8.
scout.approach: Add `pub fn record_completion(&self) { self.completion_count.increment(); }` on `ConversationMetrics`. Test it by calling the method ten times and asserting `completion_count.value() == 10` (or checking the `conversation_completion_count` snapshot if fields are kept private).
scout.notes: Only increment this for terminal success. Do not also call it for timeout/failure paths; R6 maps both `Failed` and `TimedOut` to the error counter.

files_changed.note: Adds infallible record_completion backed by CounterHandle increment.
Verifies ten completion records produce counter value 10.

dev.how: record_completion increments completion_count once per terminal success. The method is infallible and uses the monotonic atomic counter handle directly.

checklist.note: Conversation completions are counted by a global monotonically increasing counter.

stories.note: Application developers can observe completion rates from the registry snapshot.

acceptance.criterion: record_completion() increments completion_count by 1
acceptance.evidence: crates/liminal/src/metrics/conversation.rs:69-71 calls self.completion_count.increment().

acceptance.criterion: Calling record_completion 10 times results in counter value 10
acceptance.evidence: Test completion_count_tracks_successful_terminal_events at crates/liminal/tests/conversation_metrics.rs:46-64 calls record_completion ten times and asserts completion_count.value() == 10 plus snapshot Counter(10).

acceptance.criterion: record_completion is infallible
acceptance.evidence: crates/liminal/src/metrics/conversation.rs:69-71 shows record_completion returns () and has no fallible operations.

requirements.title: Track conversation duration as a histogram
requirements.spec: ConversationMetrics SHALL expose record_duration(duration: Duration) that observes the given Duration into the duration histogram. Duration is the elapsed time from conversation start to terminal state (completion, failure, or timeout). The histogram SHALL use configurable bucket boundaries provided at construction time. SHALL NOT impose default bucket values.
acceptance: record_duration(Duration::from_secs(5)) records an observation of 5 seconds
Bucket boundaries are passed at construction time, not hardcoded
record_duration is infallible
Duration is recorded in seconds as f64 for histogram observation

files: crates/liminal/src/metrics/conversation.rs:new — implement constructor bucket parameter and `record_duration` conversion here
crates/liminal/src/metrics/channel.rs:65-73 — duration conversion precedent, but it records milliseconds as `u64` and uses `unwrap_or` for saturation
crates/liminal/src/metrics/registry/collectors.rs:152-175 — histogram observations currently accept `u64` and bucket lookup is linear over `Vec<u64>`
crates/liminal/src/metrics/registry/families.rs:157-190 — histogram family stores normalized `Vec<u64>` bucket boundaries
docs/design/observability/briefs/OBS-003.json:67-75 — acceptance says seconds as `f64` and caller-supplied buckets
context: Current histogram primitives accept `u64`, not `f64`; `HistogramHandle::observe(value: u64)` and bucket boundaries are `Vec<u64>`.
Channel latency records milliseconds via `duration.as_millis()` -> `u64`; OBS-003 explicitly wants seconds, so do not copy `duration_to_millis`.
Histogram bucket boundaries are sorted/deduped by the registry; an incompatible second registration under the same name returns `MetricRegistrationError::IncompatibleHistogramBuckets`.

R8 says O(1), but current histogram observation scans bucket boundaries linearly. This is existing collector behavior; conversation code should avoid adding any extra work, but strict O(1) is not fully achievable without changing the collector, which is outside the brief.
scout.approach: Accept duration bucket boundaries in the constructor and pass them directly to `registry.register_histogram(CONVERSATION_DURATION_NAME, [], duration_buckets)?` (or equivalent if the team extends the registry to `f64`). Implement `record_duration(&self, duration: Duration)` by converting to seconds for observation; with today’s `u64` API, the least invasive implementation is a saturating integer-seconds conversion such as `u64::try_from(duration.as_secs()).unwrap_or(u64::MAX)`, but note that this does not fully satisfy the `f64` wording. If strict `f64` acceptance is expected, first extend histogram storage/snapshots from `u64` to `f64` across `collectors.rs`, `registry.rs`, and `families.rs`, but that expands the scope beyond the requested new conversation module.
scout.notes: The `Duration` conversion is the highest-risk gotcha. `as_secs_f64()` matches the spec text but cannot be passed to the current histogram API; `as_secs()` matches the existing collector but loses fractional seconds. Flag this in implementation notes/tests so reviewers understand the tradeoff.

files_changed.note: Adds record_duration(Duration), converting elapsed time with as_secs_f64 before observing the histogram.
Generalizes histogram registration and snapshots to f64 bucket boundaries.
Stores histogram boundaries as f64 and accepts generic HistogramValue observations.
Updates histogram families to support f64-compatible bucket boundaries and observations.
Adds a small conversion trait so existing integer histogram callers and new f64-second callers share the same API.
Updates existing histogram bucket snapshot assertions for f64 upper bounds.
Verifies caller-provided buckets are sorted/used and a 5-second duration lands in the 5.0-second bucket.

dev.how: record_duration records Duration values in seconds via Duration::as_secs_f64. To satisfy the f64 acceptance instead of truncating to integer seconds, the histogram primitive now stores f64 bucket boundaries in snapshots and accepts f64-compatible observations while retaining compatibility with existing integer callers.
dev.deviation: The scout identified the existing u64 histogram primitive as a conflict and offered a strict-f64 path that required extending registry/collector/family histogram storage. I chose that path, expanding changes beyond conversation.rs, so OBS-003 records seconds as f64 rather than using the scout's less-complete integer-seconds fallback.

checklist.note: Conversation duration is tracked by a global histogram using caller-supplied bucket boundaries and f64-second observations.

stories.note: Application developers can inspect conversation duration distributions without instrumenting their own code.

acceptance.criterion: record_duration(Duration::from_secs(5)) records an observation of 5 seconds
acceptance.evidence: crates/liminal/src/metrics/conversation.rs:73-75 observes duration.as_secs_f64(); test duration_records_seconds_into_configured_histogram at crates/liminal/tests/conversation_metrics.rs:66-92 records Duration::from_secs(5) and verifies the 5.0 bucket count is 1.

acceptance.criterion: Bucket boundaries are passed at construction time, not hardcoded
acceptance.evidence: crates/liminal/src/metrics/conversation.rs:34-49 accepts duration_buckets in ConversationMetrics::new and passes them to registry.register_histogram; no default bucket constants exist in conversation.rs.

acceptance.criterion: record_duration is infallible
acceptance.evidence: crates/liminal/src/metrics/conversation.rs:73-75 shows record_duration returns () and delegates to direct histogram observation.

acceptance.criterion: Duration is recorded in seconds as f64 for histogram observation
acceptance.evidence: crates/liminal/src/metrics/conversation.rs:73-75 uses Duration::as_secs_f64(); crates/liminal/src/metrics/registry.rs:316-327 normalizes histogram buckets as f64; crates/liminal/src/metrics/registry/collectors.rs:164-175 accepts HistogramValue values and observes f64.

requirements.title: Track conversation error rate as a counter
requirements.spec: ConversationMetrics SHALL expose record_error() that increments the error_count counter by 1. This is called when a conversation reaches a terminal failure state (participant crash, timeout, explicit failure). The counter SHALL be monotonically increasing.
acceptance: record_error() increments error_count by 1
Calling record_error 3 times results in counter value 3
record_error is infallible

files: crates/liminal/src/metrics/conversation.rs:new — implement `record_error` here
crates/liminal/src/metrics/registry/collectors.rs:83-111 — `CounterHandle` monotonic atomic counter behavior
crates/liminal/src/conversation/types.rs:170-183 — lifecycle phases include `Closed` and `Failed`, useful semantic mapping for later actor integration
crates/liminal/src/conversation/actor.rs:455-477 — participant crash path currently marks state failed and drains pending receives
context: `record_error` is just a counter increment; it must not attempt to classify crash/timeout/explicit failure itself.
The existing conversation actor has failure semantics (`ConversationPhase::Failed`, participant crash handling) but this brief explicitly says not to manage actor lifecycle or behavior.
Timeout handling is represented in config/deadline state but not obviously enforced in the actor yet; the metrics API should still expose the method for future call sites.
scout.approach: Add `pub fn record_error(&self) { self.error_count.increment(); }` and unit-test it with three calls yielding value 3. Keep it independent of `record_end` so callers can either record a raw error signal or use R6’s terminal helper when the conversation is actually closing.
scout.notes: Do not try to wire this into `handle_participant_exit` or deadlines in OBS-003. This brief only defines the metrics surface; actor integration may be a later stacked brief.

files_changed.note: Adds infallible record_error backed by CounterHandle increment.
Verifies three error records produce counter value 3.

dev.how: record_error increments the error_count counter for terminal failure signals. It is intentionally classification-free and infallible, leaving crash/timeout/failure mapping to callers or the terminal helper.

checklist.note: Conversation errors are counted by a global monotonically increasing counter.

stories.note: Operators can inspect the error rate to detect failing conversations.

acceptance.criterion: record_error() increments error_count by 1
acceptance.evidence: crates/liminal/src/metrics/conversation.rs:77-79 calls self.error_count.increment().

acceptance.criterion: Calling record_error 3 times results in counter value 3
acceptance.evidence: Test error_count_tracks_failed_terminal_events at crates/liminal/tests/conversation_metrics.rs:94-112 calls record_error three times and asserts error_count.value() == 3 plus snapshot Counter(3).

acceptance.criterion: record_error is infallible
acceptance.evidence: crates/liminal/src/metrics/conversation.rs:77-79 shows record_error returns () and contains no fallible operation.

requirements.title: Convenience method for terminal conversation events
requirements.spec: ConversationMetrics SHALL expose record_terminal(outcome: ConversationOutcome, duration: Duration) that atomically records the completion or error counter, the duration, and decrements the active count. ConversationOutcome SHALL be an enum with Completed, Failed, and TimedOut variants. This avoids callers forgetting to call record_end after recording a terminal event.
acceptance: ConversationOutcome has exactly three variants: Completed, Failed, TimedOut
record_terminal(Completed, dur) calls record_completion, record_duration, and record_end
record_terminal(Failed, dur) calls record_error, record_duration, and record_end
record_terminal(TimedOut, dur) calls record_error, record_duration, and record_end
record_terminal is infallible

files: crates/liminal/src/metrics/conversation.rs:new — define `ConversationOutcome` and `record_terminal` here
crates/liminal/src/conversation/types.rs:170-183 — existing phase names to avoid confusing `Closed` with metric outcome `Completed`
crates/liminal/src/conversation/actor.rs:430-448 — normal close path that will eventually map to `ConversationOutcome::Completed`
crates/liminal/src/conversation/actor.rs:455-477 — participant crash path that will eventually map to `ConversationOutcome::Failed`
context: Public enum types in this codebase derive at least `Debug`; for testability and ergonomics use `#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug, PartialEq, Eq)]` for `ConversationOutcome`.
Acceptance says exactly three variants: `Completed`, `Failed`, `TimedOut`; do not add `Cancelled`, `Closed`, or error payload variants.

“Atomically records” should be interpreted as no fallible gaps and no locks in the conversation wrapper; the current registry has separate atomic collectors, so multi-metric all-or-nothing atomicity is not available.
Order matters only for operator interpretation; a sensible order is increment completion/error, record duration, then decrement active, matching the acceptance wording.
scout.approach: Define `pub enum ConversationOutcome { Completed, Failed, TimedOut }`. Implement `pub fn record_terminal(&self, outcome: ConversationOutcome, duration: Duration)` with a `match`: `Completed` calls `record_completion()`, while `Failed | TimedOut` call `record_error()`, then call `record_duration(duration)` and `record_end()`. Unit tests should start one active conversation, call `record_terminal` for each variant with a known duration, and assert active decreases while the correct counter increments and histogram bucket count changes.
scout.notes: Because the helper decrements active count unconditionally, tests/callers should pair it with a prior `record_start`. The metrics layer should not track whether a start occurred; that would require extra state and violate the non-blocking/low-overhead spirit.

files_changed.note: Covers Completed, Failed, and TimedOut terminal mappings and active-count decrement behavior.

dev.how: Added ConversationOutcome with exactly Completed, Failed, and TimedOut variants. record_terminal maps Completed to completion_count and Failed/TimedOut to error_count, then records duration and decrements the active gauge so callers cannot omit record_end for terminal events.

checklist.note: record_terminal decrements the active gauge for terminal events.
Failed and TimedOut terminal events increment error_count.

stories.note: Operators can see active count decrease and error count increase for failed/time-out terminal events.
Application developers can correlate terminal completions with duration observations.

acceptance.criterion: ConversationOutcome has exactly three variants: Completed, Failed, TimedOut
acceptance.evidence: crates/liminal/src/metrics/conversation.rs:21-26 defines only Completed, Failed, and TimedOut; test conversation_outcome_has_expected_variants at crates/liminal/tests/conversation_metrics.rs:149-162 exhaustively matches those variants.

acceptance.criterion: record_terminal(Completed, dur) calls record_completion, record_duration, and record_end
acceptance.evidence: crates/liminal/src/metrics/conversation.rs:81-87 maps Completed to record_completion, then calls record_duration and record_end; test terminal_completed_records_completion_duration_and_end at crates/liminal/tests/conversation_metrics.rs:114-129 verifies completion=1, active=0, error=0, and duration bucket count=1.

acceptance.criterion: record_terminal(Failed, dur) calls record_error, record_duration, and record_end
acceptance.evidence: crates/liminal/src/metrics/conversation.rs:81-87 maps Failed to record_error, then calls record_duration and record_end; test terminal_failures_record_error_duration_and_end at crates/liminal/tests/conversation_metrics.rs:131-147 includes Failed and verifies active=0, error count participates in total 2, and duration bucket count participates in total 2.

acceptance.criterion: record_terminal(TimedOut, dur) calls record_error, record_duration, and record_end
acceptance.evidence: crates/liminal/src/metrics/conversation.rs:81-87 maps TimedOut to record_error, then calls record_duration and record_end; test terminal_failures_record_error_duration_and_end at crates/liminal/tests/conversation_metrics.rs:131-147 includes TimedOut and verifies active=0, error_count=2, duration bucket count=2.

acceptance.criterion: record_terminal is infallible
acceptance.evidence: crates/liminal/src/metrics/conversation.rs:81-88 shows record_terminal returns () and only invokes infallible direct recording methods.

requirements.title: Add conversation module to metrics mod.rs
requirements.spec: crates/liminal/src/metrics/mod.rs SHALL declare pub mod conversation and re-export ConversationMetrics and ConversationOutcome.

ConversationMetrics is re-exported from the metrics module
ConversationOutcome is re-exported from the metrics module

crates/liminal/src/lib.rs:1-19 — crate already exposes `pub mod metrics` and re-exports only `MetricsRegistry` at crate root
crates/liminal/src/metrics/conversation.rs:new — new module whose public types need re-exporting
crates/liminal/src/metrics/channel.rs:13-19 — existing exported metrics wrapper type style
context: `metrics/mod.rs` follows CN3: only `pub mod` declarations and `pub use` re-exports, no logic.

Adding a new metrics submodule should be localized to `metrics/mod.rs`; `lib.rs` already exposes the whole `metrics` module, and the brief does not ask for crate-root re-exports of `ConversationMetrics`.
Re-export order is currently simple: modules first, then type exports. Keep the file short and mechanical.
scout.approach: Modify `metrics/mod.rs` to add `pub mod conversation;` near `pub mod channel;`, then add `pub use conversation::{ConversationMetrics, ConversationOutcome};` before the registry re-export block. Do not add constructor logic or tests to `mod.rs`; keep it declarations and re-exports only.
scout.notes: If rustfmt reorders grouped imports elsewhere, let it. The important convention is that `mod.rs` remains logic-free.

requirements.title: Non-blocking recording guarantee
requirements.spec: All ConversationMetrics recording methods SHALL complete without acquiring any Mutex or RwLock. Recording SHALL NOT allocate heap memory on the hot path. These methods are called inside the conversation actor's message loop and SHALL NOT introduce latency into conversation processing.
acceptance: No Mutex::lock or RwLock::write appears in any recording method
No heap allocation appears in any recording method
Recording a metric completes in O(1) time

files: crates/liminal/src/metrics/conversation.rs:new — keep all recording methods as direct handle calls and simple conversions
crates/liminal/src/metrics/registry/collectors.rs:95-169 — direct handle methods are atomic and allocate no heap memory during increment/decrement/observe
crates/liminal/src/metrics/registry/families.rs:41-68 — family handle lookup can acquire `RwLock` and allocate/cache on first label use
crates/liminal/src/metrics/registry/families.rs:119-145 — gauge family has same cache/lock/allocation behavior
crates/liminal/src/metrics/registry/families.rs:192-219 — histogram family has same cache/lock/allocation behavior
context: The non-blocking guarantee is easiest to satisfy by storing direct metric handles resolved at construction time. `CounterHandle`/`GaugeHandle` methods only touch atomics; `HistogramHandle::observe` scans buckets and increments an atomic bucket count.

Family methods (`increment(label)`, `decrement(label)`, `observe(label, value)`) call `handle(label_value)`, which checks an `RwLock<HashMap<...>>` cache and may register/allocate a new labelled handle. That violates the exact R8 wording if used inside recording methods.
Avoid any formatting, string creation, `Vec` creation, label construction, or registry calls in recording methods; all registration and bucket normalization belongs in `new`.
There is no `Mutex` in the metric handles; the only locks are in registry/family construction or snapshot/cache paths.
scout.approach: Implement recording methods only in terms of already-held `GaugeHandle`, `CounterHandle`, and `HistogramHandle`. Make the methods infallible (`()`) and avoid `Result`, closures that allocate, label strings, `format!`, `to_owned`, `Vec`, and calls to `registry.register_*`. For static analysis, reviewers can grep `conversation.rs` recording methods for `lock`, `RwLock`, `Mutex`, `register_`, `to_owned`, `String`, `Vec`, and `format!`; none should appear except `Vec<u64>` in the constructor signature if using current histogram API.
scout.notes: Current `HistogramHandle::observe` is O(number_of_buckets), not mathematically O(1). If acceptance is interpreted strictly, the collector would need a different bucket lookup strategy or bounded bucket count; otherwise document that OBS-003 adds no extra non-O(1) work beyond the existing histogram primitive.

files_changed.note: All recording methods call already-resolved direct handles without registry/family lookup, label construction, lock acquisition, or heap allocation.
Histogram observation accepts f64-compatible values without hot-path allocation or locks.

dev.how: ConversationMetrics stores direct GaugeHandle, CounterHandle, and HistogramHandle values resolved at construction time. The recording methods do not call Mutex/RwLock APIs, do not allocate, do not format strings, and do not access the registry or metric families on the hot path; they only perform simple duration conversion and atomic handle operations. The existing histogram primitive still compares against configured bucket boundaries, as called out by the scout, but OBS-003 adds no lock or allocation around that primitive.
dev.deviation: The scout noted that strict mathematical O(1) histogram bucket selection is not fully achievable with the current configurable-boundary histogram primitive. I preserved the direct-handle/non-blocking design and avoided adding any locks or allocations in ConversationMetrics recording methods.

checklist.note: Active-count recording uses direct atomic gauge operations.
Completion recording uses direct atomic counter operations.
Duration recording uses a direct histogram handle with caller-supplied buckets and no registry/family lookup.
Error recording uses direct atomic counter operations.

acceptance.criterion: No Mutex::lock or RwLock::write appears in any recording method
acceptance.evidence: crates/liminal/src/metrics/conversation.rs:61-88 contains all recording methods and no Mutex/RwLock use; search of conversation.rs for Mutex/RwLock/lock/write/read/allocation-like hot-path calls returned only constructor registration lines.

acceptance.criterion: No heap allocation appears in any recording method
acceptance.evidence: crates/liminal/src/metrics/conversation.rs:61-88 recording methods only call direct handle methods and Duration::as_secs_f64; HistogramHandle::observe at crates/liminal/src/metrics/registry/collectors.rs:164-175 converts, checks finite, computes bucket index, and fetch_adds without allocation.

acceptance.criterion: Recording a metric completes in O(1) time
acceptance.evidence: Fixed during review. Histogram observations scan configured bucket boundaries at crates/liminal/src/metrics/registry/collectors.rs:72-80, but registration now caps boundaries at MAX_HISTOGRAM_BUCKETS=64 in crates/liminal/src/metrics/registry.rs:22-23 and enforces it before registration in crates/liminal/src/metrics/registry/families.rs:287-295, making observation bounded fixed work. Regression test histogram_registration_rejects_more_than_fixed_bucket_limit at crates/liminal/tests/metrics_registry.rs:85-114 verifies 65 boundaries are rejected.

issues: Original diff did not satisfy strict O(1) recording: record_duration called HistogramHandle::observe, whose bucket_index linearly scanned an unbounded configurable Vec of bucket boundaries.
fixes: Added MAX_HISTOGRAM_BUCKETS=64 and TooManyHistogramBuckets registration error.
Enforced the bucket cap before histogram family/metric registration.
Extracted registry public metric types to registry/types.rs to keep registry.rs under CN1's 500-line limit after hardening.

boundaries: SHALL NOT implement channel-specific metrics — that is OBS-002.
SHALL NOT implement pressure-related metrics — that is OBS-004.
SHALL NOT implement Prometheus text rendering — that is OBS-005.
SHALL NOT manage conversation lifecycle or actor behaviour — metrics are recorded by calling ConversationMetrics methods from the actor.
SHALL NOT impose default histogram bucket boundaries — they are caller-supplied.
SHALL NOT track per-channel conversation breakdowns — conversations are cross-cutting and counted globally.
verification: cargo clippy -p liminal -- -D warnings passes clean.
All recording methods are infallible (no Result return type).
No Mutex or RwLock acquisition in any recording method.
